planting.note: Set out only after nights stay above 50F.

directions: Start seed indoors 8 weeks before last frost in warm, moist flats and keep soil at 75-85F for reliable sprouting. Transplant into rich, well-drained soil once nights stay above 50F, spacing clumps well apart to allow fanning growth. Water consistently through the season and feed monthly with a nitrogen-rich fertilizer for lush stalks. In zones colder than 9, dig and pot up a division to overwinter indoors in a bright window. Harvest by twisting off outer stalks at the base once they reach pencil thickness.

planting.note: Needs sharp drainage to survive winter wet.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press in without covering, as light aids germination. Grow in lean, gritty soil in full sun where it forms a low aromatic mound prized in Middle Eastern spice blends. Water sparingly once established, since the plant resents soggy roots and rots in wet clay. Shear lightly after flowering to keep the habit compact and encourage fresh flavorful leaves. In cold zones, mulch the crown or overwinter a potted plant under cover.

Sow indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost, barely covering the seed and keeping flats around 75F. This tiny-leaved Italian basil naturally forms a dense round bush without much pinching. Transplant after all danger of frost into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Water evenly and harvest by clipping whole stems just above a leaf pair to keep growth bushy. Remove flower buds promptly to extend the productive leafy season.

planting.note: Direct-sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Direct-sow after the soil warms past 65F, scattering seed thinly and thinning to a hand's width apart. The bright citrus scent makes this basil superb in Southeast Asian dishes and cold drinks. Give it full sun and steady moisture in rich, free-draining soil for the best oil content. Pinch growing tips early to build a bushy plant and delay bolting. Harvest leaves in the morning when the fragrant oils are most concentrated.

planting.note: Vigorous spreader; contain in beds or pots.

directions: Plant rooted divisions in moist to boggy soil in spring, ideally in a sunken container to curb its aggressive rhizomes. This Southeast Asian herb carries a distinctive tangy, fishy-citrus aroma used raw in Vietnamese and Chinese cooking. It thrives in partial shade with constant moisture and even tolerates shallow standing water. Cut back runaway runners to keep it from overtaking a bed. Harvest young leaves and tender shoots through the growing season, and mulch crowns in colder zones.

Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and press it onto the surface without burying, since light triggers germination. This improved culinary strain offers sweeter, milder flavor than wild oregano relatives. Transplant to a sunny, well-drained bed once frost danger passes, spacing plants closely. Pinch tips regularly to promote branching and harvest just as flower buds form for peak aroma. Bring a potted plant indoors over winter in colder climates.

Surface-sow seed indoors 6 weeks before last frost and keep warm and bright until sprouts appear. The gold-and-red buds contain spilanthol, which produces a numbing, tingling sensation when chewed. Transplant into rich, moist soil in full sun after frost, spacing plants for their sprawling habit. Deadhead spent buds to keep new ones coming and pinch stems to encourage bushiness. Harvest flower buds fresh for medicinal use or to add a buzzing zing to salads.

Direct-sow in early spring onto lean, sandy soil, as the taproot dislikes disturbance and transplanting. The whole plant is used in traditional herbalism as a mild sedative and sleep aid. Give it full sun and minimal water, since rich, moist ground produces weak, floppy growth. Thin seedlings to prevent crowding and let some flowers set seed for reliable self-sowing. Harvest the aerial parts when in bloom and dry them for tea or tincture.

Sow seed in fall so winter cold provides the stratification needed to break dormancy. This woodland perennial forms a large clump valued for its aromatic roots used in traditional respiratory remedies. Plant in humus-rich, moist soil in dappled shade at the edge of a woodland garden. Be patient, as roots may take several years to reach harvestable size. Mulch generously each autumn and divide mature clumps to propagate.

planting.note: Surface-sow; needs light and cold to germinate.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed in fall onto moist ground, pressing it in without covering so light and cold can trigger sprouting. Boneset is a native wetland perennial long used in folk medicine for fevers and colds. Plant it in consistently damp to wet soil in full sun to light shade, ideal for rain gardens. The white flat-topped flower clusters draw many pollinators in late summer. Harvest the leaves and flowering tops as blooms open and dry them thoroughly.

Sow fresh seed in fall in a shaded woodland bed, expecting slow, erratic germination over one or two seasons. This native perennial has a long history in traditional medicine, though the berries are toxic if eaten. Provide rich, moist, humusy soil in full to partial shade beneath deciduous trees. Keep the site mulched with leaf litter and never let it dry out during summer. Harvest the roots only from well-established, multi-year clumps.

Sow in fall or cold-stratify seed for four weeks before spring sowing to break dormancy. This drought-tough native milkweed has a deep taproot historically used for respiratory ailments. Plant in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun and avoid moving it once established. The brilliant orange flowers are a magnet for butterflies and native bees. Water only during severe drought and harvest roots from mature plants in autumn.

Surface-sow the dust-fine seed in fall, leaving it uncovered so cold and light can trigger germination. This tall native perennial was used by Indigenous peoples and early settlers as a liver remedy. Grow it in moist, fertile soil in full sun to light shade where the white flower spires shine in summer. Keep the roots evenly moist, as dry soil causes lower leaves to scorch. Harvest roots from established plants in autumn and dry for at least a year before use.

Plant rhizome divisions in spring in rich, moist woodland soil where the twining vine can climb a trellis or shrub. This native climber has a long history in traditional herbalism for the compounds in its roots. Give it partial shade and a support structure, as stems can reach many feet in a season. Keep soil consistently moist and mulch the crown heavily each fall. Harvest the knobby rhizomes from established plants after two or more growing seasons.

Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, surface-pressing it and keeping flats near 78F. This African and Asian shrubby basil carries a warm clove-like scent used medicinally and in cooking. Transplant to warm, fertile soil in full sun after frost, giving each plant ample room to bush out. Pinch flowering tips to prolong leaf production and improve branching. In cool climates grow it as a container plant that can overwinter indoors.

Sow seed indoors 6 weeks before last frost, barely covering it and keeping flats warm. This anise-scented marigold serves as a tarragon substitute and yields golden fall flowers for tea. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil once the weather settles and nights warm. It tolerates heat and drought better than French tarragon, thriving where that herb fails. Pinch stems for bushier growth and harvest leaves through the season, drying flowers for winter tea.

Start seed indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost, covering lightly and keeping soil near 75F. This large-leaved Italian classic is the standard for pesto and Mediterranean cooking. Transplant into warm, rich soil in full sun only after all frost danger passes. Pinch out the growing tips at six leaves to force a bushy, high-yielding plant. Harvest whole stems above a leaf node and remove flower buds promptly to keep leaves tender and sweet.

Sow indoors 4-6 weeks ahead of the last frost and keep flats warm and bright to preserve rich color. This deep-purple basil adds dramatic color to vinegars, salads, and garnishes. Transplant after frost into full sun, where strong light intensifies the burgundy pigment. Pinch regularly to build a dense plant and prevent early flowering. Harvest young leaves for the most vivid color and infuse them into oils or vinegars.

planting.note: Best grown from cuttings; contain roots.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings or divisions, since named mints do not come true from seed. This citrusy peppermint variety perfumes teas, desserts, and cocktails with orange-bergamot notes. Plant in moist, rich soil in partial shade and confine roots to a buried pot or bottomless container. Water frequently and shear plants back after flowering to renew fresh leafy growth. Divide clumps every two or three years to keep the patch vigorous.

Start banana mint from divisions or cuttings, as it will not reproduce true from seed. Its soft fuzzy leaves carry a sweet fruity aroma prized for desserts and beverages. Grow in consistently moist, fertile soil in dappled sun, keeping roots contained to stop rampant spreading. Water generously in hot weather and cut plants back hard mid-season to renew tender foliage. Lift and divide crowded clumps every couple of years.

Grow this variegated thyme from cuttings or division to keep the silver-edged leaf pattern true. It offers standard culinary thyme flavor along with attractive frosty foliage. Plant in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun and water sparingly once established. Trim after flowering to prevent woodiness and encourage fresh compact growth. Harvest sprigs any time and mulch the crown in cold-winter regions.

Propagate from cuttings to preserve the distinct orange-balsam fragrance of this thyme. The narrow grey-green leaves lend a citrusy note to poultry, fish, and marinades. Set plants in lean, fast-draining soil in full sun and keep water to a minimum. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain a tidy mound and prevent the base from going woody. Harvest sprigs throughout the season and protect crowns with gravel mulch over winter.

Start seed indoors 6 weeks before last frost, covering lightly and keeping flats warm. This anise-flavored marigold thrives in heat and humidity where true tarragon struggles. Transplant into full sun in well-drained soil once nights stay warm, and pinch to encourage branching. Harvest leaves through the season for culinary use in place of French tarragon. In frost-free zones it behaves as a perennial, blooming golden flowers in fall.

Surface-sow indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, pressing seed onto the mix without covering it. This East Asian herb has minty-anise leaves used in Korean cooking and herbal teas. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, where purple flower spikes draw abundant bees. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and allow a few seed heads to ripen for self-sowing. Harvest young leaves through summer and cut plants back in fall.

Direct-sow in early spring in cool, moist soil, covering seed lightly and thinning to a hand's width. The striking red-veined leaves add tangy lemon flavor and ornamental color to salads. Grow in partial shade with steady moisture to keep leaves tender and slow bolting. Remove flower stalks to prolong leaf harvest and prevent aggressive self-seeding. Cut leaves young for the best texture and divide crowns every few years.

Direct-sow thickly in cool weather, barely covering the seed, which sprouts within days. This peppery quick crop is harvested young as a spicy microgreen or baby salad green. Sow in succession every couple of weeks and keep the soil evenly moist to slow bolting. Provide partial shade in warm spells to extend the harvest window. Snip seedlings with scissors just above the soil once they reach a few inches tall.

Start seed indoors 8 weeks before last frost, sowing in clusters and keeping soil around 65F. This garlic chive selection is grown especially for its tender edible flower stalks used in stir-fries. Transplant clumps to full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly watered. Harvest the round flower buds and stems just before they open for the mildest flavor. Divide the perennial clumps every few years and cut back foliage after frost.

Direct-sow in early spring in well-drained soil, covering seed lightly and thinning young plants. This Italian wild green, also called bladder campion, has a mild sweet-grassy flavor for risottos and omelets. Grow in full sun with moderate water where it forms tender leafy rosettes. Harvest young shoots and leaves before flowering for the best texture and taste. Let a few plants flower to self-sow for a continuing patch.

Surface-sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost, leaving seed uncovered since light aids germination. This golden-leaved anise hyssop offers licorice-scented foliage for teas and blue flower spikes for pollinators. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, spacing to allow good airflow. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering into fall and prevent excessive self-seeding. Harvest leaves and flowers fresh or dried and cut plants back after frost.

Surface-sow indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost, keeping the fine seed moist and lightly covered. This leafy celery is grown for its strongly flavored foliage rather than thick stalks. Transplant to rich, consistently moist soil in full sun to partial shade and never let it dry out. Harvest outer stems and leaves as needed, and the plant will keep producing all season. In mild winters it survives to provide an early spring cut, and it stores well dried.
